title: Secrets Management Quiz
description: Test your knowledge on Vaults, dynamic secrets, and avoiding hardcoded credentials.

questions.question: Which of the following describes an severe anti-pattern for handling API keys?

options.text: Injecting them into the application via a secure CI/CD pipeline.
Committing them to a private GitHub repository alongside the source code.
Fetching them dynamically from AWS Secrets Manager on application boot.
Storing them in a Vault with strict access policies.

questions.question: How does a system like AWS Secrets Manager or HashiCorp Vault securely provide a password to an application?

options.text: It emails the password to the lead developer daily.
It writes the password to a public S3 bucket temporarily.
The application authenticates itself to the Vault upon boot, and the Vault serves the decrypted secret directly into the application's memory over a secure connection.
It hardcodes the password into the Docker image during the build process.

questions.question: What is the massive security advantage of utilizing 'Dynamic Secrets' as implemented by HashiCorp Vault?

options.text: They prevent all DDoS attacks.
They are completely free to use.
Instead of returning a static password, the Vault creates a brand-new set of credentials with a short time-to-live (TTL) that automatically expire, rendering stolen credentials useless very quickly.
They use biometric fingerprint scanners to authorize database access.

questions.question: If your team uses GitOps and you must store Kubernetes Secrets via YAML files in your Git repository, how do you do this securely?

options.text: By using tools like Bitnami Sealed Secrets to asymmetrically encrypt the YAML file before committing, relying on a private key inside the cluster to decrypt it.
By just Base64 encoding the values.
By keeping the repository strictly private, as that provides perfect security.
By deleting the Git history every 30 days.

questions.question: Why is storing secrets in a plain-text local `.env` file on a production server dangerous?

options.text: The `.env` file limits the application's RAM usage.
Node.js cannot read `.env` files efficiently.
AWS automatically deletes files named `.env`.
If a vulnerability like a Directory Traversal attack exists in the app, hackers can simply instruct the server to read and return the file's contents.
